summary: My early inspiration to create art came from books by F. Hopkinson Smith. His beautifully illustrated works sparked my imagination and introduced me to traditional artists' tools. These books played a significant role in shaping my artistic journey from a young age.

I had an impressive boyhood trip to Chicago that left a lasting impression on me. That experience was formative and helped broaden my perspective on art and culture. Chicago's vibrant environment contributed to my early artistic development.

Vanity Fair was one of the beautifully illustrated magazines that influenced my notion of creating art. Its rich visual content and high-quality illustrations inspired me to explore different artistic mediums. The magazine was part of the cultural backdrop during my formative years.

Olivet College was a place I was connected to, notable for its celebrated visitors. The college environment exposed me to a variety of artistic and intellectual influences that enriched my understanding of art. It played a role in my early education and artistic formation.

Joseph Brewer's collections of art and art books were an important resource for me. His assemblage provided access to a wide range of artistic ideas and historical references that influenced my work. I valued his role in supporting and preserving art collections.

During the 1940s, I was involved with Bay Area artists, which was a vibrant and creative community. This period was marked by experimentation and working in several mediums concurrently. The Bay Area scene was crucial in shaping my artistic approach and network.

In 1951, I had a solo exhibition at the Santa Barbara Museum of Art, which was a significant milestone in my career. This opportunity allowed me to showcase my evolving work, especially as I began to diminish my use of oils. The museum was an important venue for my artistic expression.

I acquired a second collection of antique papers in Florence, which deepened my interest in collage as a favored medium. The city's rich artistic heritage inspired me to explore new materials and techniques. Florence was pivotal in the development of my collage work.

I worked with the Geddis-Martin Gallery in Santa Barbara, which helped me present my work to a wider audience. Collaborating with this gallery was part of my broader engagement with galleries that supported my artistic career. They were instrumental in promoting my exhibitions locally.

The Duveen-Graham Gallery in New York was one of the key galleries I worked with during my career. This connection gave me access to the New York art scene and helped me reach a more diverse audience. It was an important part of my professional network.

Edith Halpert's Downtown Gallery in New York was another significant venue where I exhibited my work. The gallery was known for its support of modern artists, and working with them helped establish my presence in the art world. It was a valuable relationship in my career.

Bertha Lewinson in Los Angeles was one of the dealers I worked with, helping to promote and sell my art. Her support was important in expanding my reach on the West Coast. I appreciated her dedication to representing my work effectively.

Esther Bear in Santa Barbara was another key figure who supported my artistic endeavors. Her involvement helped me connect with collectors and audiences in that region. Working with her was part of my broader network of galleries and dealers.

Rex Evans in Los Angeles was among the dealers I collaborated with to exhibit and sell my work. His role was significant in maintaining my presence in the Los Angeles art scene. I valued his partnership in advancing my career.

Jodi Scully, based in Los Angeles, was one of the art dealers I worked with. Her support helped me navigate the local art market and reach new audiences. Collaborating with her was beneficial to my artistic exposure.

The Staempfli Gallery in New York was part of the network of galleries that exhibited my work. Their involvement helped me maintain a presence in the New York art world. It was an important venue for showcasing my evolving artistic style.

I worked with the Springer Gallery in Berlin, which expanded my reach into the European art market. This connection was valuable in gaining international exposure and engaging with a broader audience. The gallery played a key role in my career abroad.

The Sagittarius Gallery in Rome was one of the galleries that supported my work internationally. Exhibiting there allowed me to connect with the Italian art scene and collectors. It was a meaningful part of my global artistic presence.

Antonio Souza in Mexico City was a dealer I worked with, helping me reach audiences in Mexico. His support was important for expanding my artistic influence in Latin America. Collaborating with him was part of my international gallery relationships.

Mexico City was a significant place where I connected with the art world through Antonio Souza. Exhibiting and working there helped me broaden my cultural and artistic horizons. The city was part of my international artistic journey.

Neil McRoberts in London was one of the dealers I collaborated with to present my work in the UK. His involvement helped me establish a presence in the London art market. I appreciated his role in supporting my career overseas.

London was an important city for me as I worked with Neil McRoberts to exhibit my art there. The city's vibrant art scene provided opportunities for international exposure and networking. London was a key location in my global artistic outreach.

Kurt Schwitters was a major influence on my collage work, especially regarding scale and material use. His innovative approach to collage inspired me to explore new techniques and rethink traditional art forms. Schwitters's work remains a significant reference point in my artistic practice.

Pablo Picasso's collage work was among the important influences I studied. His ability to integrate different materials and create new visual languages inspired my own experiments with collage. Picasso's art helped justify and shape my approach to mixed media.

Georges Braque's collage work was a key influence on my artistic development. His pioneering techniques in Cubism and collage encouraged me to explore materials and composition in new ways. Braque's legacy informed my approach to creating descriptive and material-oriented images.

Max Ernst's collage art was among the works that inspired me to pursue collage as a favored medium. His surreal and experimental style opened possibilities for me to work with antique papers and diverse materials. Ernst's influence is evident in my evolving artistic procedures.

Anne Ryan was one of the collage artists whose work I admired and studied. Her approach to collage helped me understand the potential of paper and materials as compositional elements. Ryan's art contributed to my appreciation of collage traditions.

Alberto Burri's work was influential in shaping my materials-oriented approach to collage. His innovative use of texture and unconventional materials encouraged me to experiment beyond traditional painting. Burri's art helped justify my shift away from descriptive images.

Jean Varda was among the collage artists whose work I respected and drew inspiration from. His playful and inventive use of collage techniques informed my own artistic explorations. Varda's contributions to collage traditions enriched my understanding of the medium.

Joseph Cornell's collage and assemblage work influenced my approach to combining materials and found objects. His poetic and imaginative style encouraged me to think about collage as a narrative and compositional tool. Cornell's art remains a significant reference in my work.

Saul Steinberg was one of the artists whose collage work I studied and admired. His unique blend of drawing and collage inspired me to consider the role of printed or written words as compositional elements. Steinberg's art helped expand my understanding of collage possibilities.
